county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Little River County formed 1867 from Hempstead and Sevier counties. County seat is Ashdown. Located in far southwest Arkansas, bordering both Oklahoma and Texas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Little River County records at the County Clerk's office in Ashdown are complete from 1867. This far southwest Arkansas county borders both Oklahoma and Texas, and cross-border research is essential. The Little River County Historical Society maintains research files. The county was part of the Choctaw removal territory and Choctaw Nation records at the Oklahoma Historical Society are relevant. Timber was a major industry, and lumber company records document many families. The Ashdown Public Library has local history materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Hempstead and Sevier county records for pre-1867 families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Little River County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Ashdown holds deed books from 1867

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Logan County formed 1871 from Franklin, Johnson, Scott, and Yell counties. County seat is Paris (northern district); Booneville serves the southern district. Magazine Mountain, the highest point in Arkansas, is in Logan County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Logan County records at the County Clerk's office in Paris are complete from 1871. The county has a dual-courthouse system with Booneville serving as the seat for the southern district. Magazine Mountain, the highest point in Arkansas, is in Logan County. The county was a coal mining area in the late 1800s and early 1900s, attracting Italian, Eastern European, and other immigrant workers. Check naturalization records at the Circuit Clerk's office. The Logan County Historical Society maintains research files. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Ozark National Forest records at NARA may document families in the forest reserve areas.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Logan County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Paris holds deed books from 1871

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lonoke County formed 1873 from Prairie and Pulaski counties. County seat is Lonoke. Located in central Arkansas east of Little Rock. Site of the first rice farm in Arkansas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lonoke County records at the County Clerk's office in Lonoke are complete from 1873. This central Arkansas county east of Little Rock was a rice-farming area (the first rice farm in Arkansas was in Lonoke County). The Lonoke County Museum has local history materials. The county's location along the railroad made it a transportation hub. Rice farming attracted both American and immigrant families, and agricultural records are important. The Lonoke County Genealogical Society maintains research files. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Pulaski and Prairie county records for pre-1873 families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Lonoke County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Lonoke holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.record_losses: 1864 Civil War guerrilla raids destroyed courthouse and records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Madison County formed 1836 from Washington County. County seat is Huntsville. Located in the Ozark Mountains. The 1864 Civil War guerrilla raids destroyed the courthouse and records. Strongly Unionist during the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Madison County's courthouse was destroyed during Civil War guerrilla raids in 1864, and records were lost. The County Clerk's office in Huntsville holds post-war records. This Ozark county was strongly Unionist, and the War Eagle community and the Battle of Prairie Grove (in adjacent Washington County) directly affected Madison County families. The Madison County Genealogical and Historical Society in Huntsville has reconstructed records and family files. The county's isolation in the Ozarks preserved traditional folkways documented by the Ozark folklore collections at the University of Arkansas. FamilySearch has microfilmed post-war county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Madison County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Huntsville holds deed books from 1846

source_profiles.survival_notes: Early deed books damaged or destroyed; 1864 Civil War guerrilla raids destroyed courthouse and records. Post-loss records at County Clerk's office are generally complete

county_metadata.record_losses: 1864 Civil War; courthouse burned by guerrillas; most records lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Marion County formed 1836 from Izard County. County seat is Yellville. Located in the Ozarks of north-central Arkansas. The 1864 Civil War courthouse burning destroyed most records. Predominantly Unionist during the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Marion County's courthouse was burned by guerrillas during the Civil War in 1864, and most records were lost. The County Clerk's office in Yellville holds post-war records. This Ozark county in north-central Arkansas was predominantly Unionist and suffered severe guerrilla warfare. The town of Yellville was named for Archibald Yell, first congressman from Arkansas. The Marion County Historical Society and the Flippin Public Library have local materials. Bull Shoals Lake (built 1951) inundated some communities. For pre-war records, check Izard County (parent) and GLO land patents. FamilySearch has microfilmed post-war county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Marion County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Yellville holds deed books from 1846

county_metadata.record_losses: Complex jurisdictional history; abolished 1838 (territory claimed by Texas), re-formed 1874; early records largely lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Miller County formed 1820 from Hempstead County, abolished 1838 (territory claimed by Republic of Texas), re-formed 1874. County seat is Texarkana. Has the most complex jurisdictional history of any Arkansas county due to the Texas boundary dispute.
county_metadata.research_tips: Miller County has a complex jurisdictional history: originally formed 1820, abolished 1838 when the area was claimed by the Republic of Texas, and re-formed 1874. Early records are largely lost. The County Clerk's office in Texarkana holds records from 1874. Texarkana straddles the Arkansas-Texas border, and researchers must check records in both states (Miller County, AR and Bowie County, TX). The Texarkana Museum System and the Southwest Arkansas Regional Archives (SARA) have regional materials. The Four States Auto Museum Research Center holds genealogy collections. For the 1820-1838 period, check Texas Republic records and NARA territorial papers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Miller County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Texarkana holds deed books from 1830

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Mississippi County formed 1833 from Crittenden County. County seat is Blytheville (changed from Osceola). Northeasternmost county in Arkansas, bordered by the Mississippi River and Missouri.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mississippi County records at the County Clerk's office in Blytheville are complete from 1833, though some early records suffered damage. This is the northeasternmost county in Arkansas, bordered by the Mississippi River and the Missouri state line. Osceola served as an earlier county seat. The county's rich delta soil made it a major cotton and later soybean area. The Blytheville Public Library has local history materials. Freedmen's Bureau records are essential for African American research. The New Madrid earthquakes of 1811-1812 significantly altered the landscape before the county was formed. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Mississippi County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Blytheville holds deed books from 1833

county_metadata.record_losses: 1860 fire destroyed some records; most deed books and probate records survived
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Monroe County formed 1829 from Arkansas and Phillips counties. County seat is Clarendon, on the White River. The 1860 fire and Civil War losses destroyed some records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Monroe County lost some records in an 1860 fire and suffered additional Civil War losses. The County Clerk's office in Clarendon holds surviving records. Clarendon, on the White River, was a steamboat town and later a railroad junction. The county spans both delta lowlands and higher ground, creating diverse settlement patterns. The Monroe County Historical Society maintains research files. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are important for African American research. The county has strong plantation records in estate inventories. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records. Check parent county records (Arkansas, Phillips) for early families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Monroe County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Clarendon holds deed books from 1829

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Montgomery County formed 1842 from Hot Spring County. County seat is Mount Ida. Located in the Ouachita Mountains. Remote and sparsely populated with a mining and farming economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Montgomery County records at the County Clerk's office in Mount Ida are complete from 1842. This remote Ouachita Mountains county was a mining area -- Crater of Diamonds (now a state park) is in adjacent Pike County but Montgomery County had gold and mineral mining. The county's isolation preserved its rural character. Small church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations are primary sources. The Montgomery County Historical Society maintains local research files. Cemetery transcriptions document many family burial grounds. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Ouachita National Forest records at NARA may document families in the forest areas.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Montgomery County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Mount Ida holds deed books from 1842

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Montgomery County churches and family burial grounds; Arkansas Gravestone Project and Find A Grave provide online access; local genealogical societies have published transcription volumes

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Nevada County formed 1871 from Columbia, Hempstead, and Ouachita counties. County seat is Prescott. Named for the Sierra Nevada, not the state. Located in southwest Arkansas in the cotton and timber region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Nevada County records at the County Clerk's office in Prescott are complete from 1871. This southwest Arkansas county was in the cotton and timber region. The county was named for the Sierra Nevada mountain range, not the state of Nevada. The Prescott-Nevada County Library has local history materials. Timber industry records from the late 1800s are important for tracking families. Probate records contain enslaved persons inventories from parent county records. The Southwest Arkansas Regional Archives (SARA) holds regional materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Hempstead, Columbia, and Ouachita county records for pre-1871 families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Nevada County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Prescott holds deed books from 1871

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada County Deed Books -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Nevada County Clerk, Prescott / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Nevada County recording land conveyances, mortgages, and property transfers; County Clerk's office is the primary repository; Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1871; housed at County Clerk's office in Prescott

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Nevada County; slave schedules document the significant slaveholding in southwest Arkansas

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada County Probate Records -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Nevada County Clerk, Prescott / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, administrations, and estate records for Nevada County; includes wills, estate inventories, guardianships, and administrator bonds

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Nevada County Clerk, Prescott / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Nevada County including bonds, licenses, and certificates; County Clerk's office is the repository

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada County Federal Land Patents -- BLM / GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (original entries) for Nevada County from the General Land Office; Arkansas is a public land state and most original land titles derive from federal patents; searchable by county and patentee name at BLM website

source_profiles.source_name: Nevada County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Arkansas History Commission / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Nevada County area; legal notices, obituaries, marriage and death announcements, estate settlements, and community news; Chronicling America has some digitized Arkansas papers
